passages.passage_text: Directions (Example Nos. 24-28) In the questions that follow, the symbols are used as follows. A © B means A is greater than B. A = B means A is equal to B. A @ B means A is either smaller than or equal to B. A ⊕ B means A is either greater than or equal to B. A @ B means A is smaller than B. Now, in each of the following questions, assuming the three statements to be true, state which of the two Conclusions I and II given below is definitely true. Give answer (a) if only Conclusion I is true (b) if only Conclusion II is true (c) if either Conclusion I or II is true (d) if neither Conclusion I nor II is true (e) if both Conclusions I and II are true

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Statements: Q @ R, R @ M, M © D Conclusions: I. D © R II. D @ Q

choices.a: if only Conclusion I is true
choices.b: if only Conclusion II is true
choices.c: if either Conclusion I or II is true
choices.d: if neither Conclusion I nor II is true
choices.e: if both Conclusions I and II are true

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Statements: M @ K, K © R, R © P © D Conclusions: I. P @ K II. P @ M

choices.a: if only Conclusion I is true
choices.b: if only Conclusion II is true
choices.c: if either Conclusion I or II is true
choices.d: if neither Conclusion I nor II is true
choices.e: if both Conclusions I and II are true

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Statements: T © M, M = P, P © R Conclusions: I. R @ T II. T © R

choices.a: if only Conclusion I is true
choices.b: if only Conclusion II is true
choices.c: if either Conclusion I or II is true
choices.d: if neither Conclusion I nor II is true
choices.e: if both Conclusions I and II are true

questions.subject: verbal
questions.body: Statements: P @ Q, Q © K, K@ M Conclusions: I. M = Q II. M ⊕ Q

choices.a: if only Conclusion I is true
choices.b: if only Conclusion II is true
choices.c: if either Conclusion I or II is true
choices.d: if neither Conclusion I nor II is true
choices.e: if both Conclusions I and II are true

solutions.solution: According to the question, Q @ R ⇒ Q < R, R @ M ⇒ R < M, M © D ⇒ M > D. On combining the statements (i), (ii) and (iii), we get Q < R < M > D. Conclusions I. D © R ⇒ D > R (false), II. D @ Q ⇒ D < Q (false). So, it is clear that neither Conclusion I nor II is true.

According to the question, M @ K ⇒ M < K, K © R ⇒ K > R, R © P © D ⇒ R > P > D. On combining the statements (i), (ii) and (iii), we get M < K > R > P. Conclusions I. P @ K ⇒ P < K (true), II. P @ M ⇒ P < M (false). So, it is clear that only Conclusion I is true.

According to the question, T © M ⇒ T > M, M = P ⇒ M = P, P © R ⇒ P > R. On combining the statements (i), (ii) and (iii), we get T > M = P > R. Conclusions I. R @ T ⇒ R < T (true), II. T © R ⇒ T > R (true). So, it is clear that both Conclusions I and II are true.

According to the question, P @ Q ⇒ P < Q, Q © K ⇒ Q > K, K @ M ⇒ K < M. On combining the statements (i), (ii) and (iii), we get P < Q > K < M. Conclusions I. M = Q ⇒ M = Q (false), II. M ⊕ Q ⇒ M ≥ Q (false). So, it is clear that neither Conclusion I nor II is true.
